speaker: Ms. Tanaka
description: Voice-reference dialogue samples for Ms. Tanaka in Pig & Swine RPG. These are draft samples, not final committed game text.
source_role_summary: Grounded professional contrast to Mr. Swine's chaos

voice_profile.core_voice: precise, professional, patient, calm, quietly dry, respectful, legally careful
comedy_engine: treats Swine's chaos with restrained professional patience
explains ambiguity calmly while others panic
uses exact distinctions between courtesy, intention, authority, and binding commitment
makes dry observations without becoming sarcastic or cruel
lets the comedy target be Swine's overconfidence, not Japan or translation
do: make her explanation of translation ambiguity central to Chapter 4
let her correct overstatements by both sides
give her dry but restrained humour
keep cultural material careful, specific, and non-stereotyped
do_not: make her speak in broken English
make her a partisan advocate for Swine

relationship_dynamics.Mr. Swine: patiently clarifies his misunderstanding without endorsing his carelessness
relationship_dynamics.Dr. A. Cula: helps him convert ambiguity into legal evidence
relationship_dynamics.Mr. Murrow: respects his careful structuring and provides precise factual distinctions
relationship_dynamics.Crab: aligns with his need for exact wording, dates, and versions
relationship_dynamics.Whimsy: politely limits his metaphors while appreciating his rights instincts
relationship_dynamics.Asia: natural professional respect; both are quietly keeping chaos usable
relationship_dynamics.Resort Counsel: corrects overconfident translation claims without advocating emotionally

notes: Every line is a voice-reference draft for later rewriting into godot/data/dialogues/dialogues.json.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Translation is not magic. It is disciplined attention to meaning, context, and consequence.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: A courteous phrase may express cooperation without creating a binding legal obligation.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Ambiguity is not always accidental. Sometimes people later discover it is useful.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: I can tell you what the words can reasonably mean. I cannot make Mr. Swine's judgment better in retrospect.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Please separate three questions: what was said, what was understood, and what was legally agreed.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Politeness can be evidence of atmosphere. It is not usually evidence of authority.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: A ceremony may make a document memorable. It does not automatically make it binding.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Words acquire legal force through context, authority, and intention, not through embarrassment.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: I am not here to rescue anyone from poor document hygiene.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: A translation note should reduce confusion, not replace legal analysis.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The problem is not that the phrase is foreign. The problem is that someone is now pretending it was exact.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: If both sides quote only the convenient part, the full sentence usually becomes important.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: A good interpreter says when a term is uncertain. Certainty can be more dangerous than doubt.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The most expensive misunderstandings are often the ones everyone smiled through.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Precision is not pedantry when a bill has many zeroes.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: generic idle at Embassy / translation office
text: Please wait here. The document scanner is more reliable than the coffee machine, which is unusual.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: generic idle at Embassy / translation office
text: I have prepared copies of the original wording, the literal translation, and the practical meaning.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: generic idle at Embassy / translation office
text: Mr. Swine is in the waiting area attempting to look legally harmless.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: generic idle at Embassy / translation office
text: If you hear a sigh from the corridor, that is probably a contract discovering context.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: generic idle at Embassy / translation office
text: Please do not write on the original document. I say this because someone always believes their pen is helpful.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: generic idle at Embassy / translation office
text: The phrase is not impossible. It is merely doing too much work for one sentence.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: generic idle at Embassy / translation office
text: I will explain the ambiguity slowly, because speed is how Mr. Swine arrived here.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: generic idle at Embassy / translation office
text: The embassy is not taking sides. The language, however, is not as confident as the invoice.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: generic idle at Embassy / translation office
text: If a document arrives with a ribbon, remove the ribbon before analysing it.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: generic idle at Embassy / translation office
text: The translation table is clear. The facts are not.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Compare the version Mr. Swine saw with the version now relied on.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Ask whether the phrase indicated courtesy, cooperation, or commitment.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Do not rely on one translated word when the legal issue concerns the whole exchange.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Find the context: who spoke, who signed, who had authority, and what was explained.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: If the resort says the meaning was obvious, ask why later clarification was necessary.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: If Mr. Swine says he felt reassured, ask what exactly reassured him.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The translation note helps most when paired with the earlier draft.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Ambiguity supports doubt. It does not prove innocence by itself.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The invoice may show how the resort interpreted the event later. It does not prove what was agreed then.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: In arbitration, avoid saying 'mistranslation' if the better point is 'overconfident interpretation.'
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: first meeting at Embassy / translation office
text: Good afternoon. I understand you need assistance with a disputed bilingual document.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: first meeting at Embassy / translation office
text: Please sit. And please give me the document before Mr. Swine explains it from memory.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: first meeting at Embassy / translation office
text: I have reviewed the phrase marked in red, the signature page, and the surrounding correspondence.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: first meeting at Embassy / translation office
text: The wording is not meaningless. It is also not as definite as the claimant appears to suggest.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: first meeting at Embassy / translation office
text: Mr. Swine, I will be precise. This may be uncomfortable, but less expensive than optimism.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: first meeting at Embassy / translation office
text: Dr. A. Cula, your legal question is not simply what the phrase says. It is what obligation could reasonably be understood from it.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: first meeting at Embassy / translation office
text: I can provide a translation note. I cannot provide a time machine for the signing ceremony.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: first meeting at Embassy / translation office
text: The key distinction is between promotional cooperation and binding commercial commitment.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: first meeting at Embassy / translation office
text: The phrase sits closer to cooperation in principle than to exclusive paid partnership.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: first meeting at Embassy / translation office
text: That does not make the matter harmless. It makes the claimant's certainty overstated.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Mr. Swine, 'the room felt non-binding' is not a translation category.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Dessert is context. It is rarely authority.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Your memory is vivid where the pudding is concerned and less vivid where the clause begins.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: You may have understood the event as ceremonial. The document should still have been read.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: A polite smile after a sentence does not erase the sentence.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: I believe you were confused. I do not believe confusion is a filing system.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: You are not the first person to mistake hospitality for reassurance.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Please stop saying the ribbon looked friendly. Ribbons do not give legal advice.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Your misunderstanding is plausible. Your confidence at the time was not advisable.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The law may forgive ambiguity. It rarely rewards failure to ask a second question.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: guiding Dr. Cula's legal use of translation note
text: Dr. A. Cula, use the note to challenge certainty, not to pretend there was no document.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: guiding Dr. Cula's legal use of translation note
text: The translation supports ambiguity. It does not erase every obligation.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: guiding Dr. Cula's legal use of translation note
text: Your strongest point is that the claimant's interpretation was not the only reasonable one.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: guiding Dr. Cula's legal use of translation note
text: If you combine the note with the earlier draft, the alleged exclusive commitment becomes much weaker.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: guiding Dr. Cula's legal use of translation note
text: Ask what was explained before signature, not only what was printed after.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: guiding Dr. Cula's legal use of translation note
text: Authority matters separately. Even a clear phrase would not automatically bind Pig & Swine without authority.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: guiding Dr. Cula's legal use of translation note
text: Do not let the claimant collapse courtesy, cooperation, and contract into one word.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: guiding Dr. Cula's legal use of translation note
text: The tribunal will appreciate careful distinction more than theatrical mistranslation claims.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: guiding Dr. Cula's legal use of translation note
text: Say: the wording was capable of a non-binding promotional meaning in context.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: guiding Dr. Cula's legal use of translation note
text: Then show why Mr. Swine's understanding, careless as it was, was not invented after the invoice.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Yes, Mr. Murrow. I would separate translation ambiguity from authority to bind.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The wording alone cannot decide the full case.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: A reasonable reader would need context: event purpose, prior draft, explanation, and conduct after signature.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The phrase can support promotional cooperation. It is not naturally a complete payment obligation.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The claimant's version appears to add commercial certainty after the event.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: I can confirm linguistic ambiguity. The legal consequences are for the tribunal.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The earlier draft is important because it shows what was expected before the disputed wording hardened.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The invoice is also important because it reveals the claimant's later interpretation.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: I would not overstate the note. Its strength is restraint.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: A careful argument will be more persuasive than a dramatic one.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: responding to Crab's exact wording/date questions
text: Yes, the date of this version matters.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: responding to Crab's exact wording/date questions
text: The phrase in the earlier draft is softer.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: responding to Crab's exact wording/date questions
text: The later version uses a term closer to undertaking, but the surrounding sentence still remains promotional.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: responding to Crab's exact wording/date questions
text: The invoice language is much firmer than the signed wording.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: responding to Crab's exact wording/date questions
text: Crab is right to ask for the sequence. Meaning changes when drafts change.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: responding to Crab's exact wording/date questions
text: The claimant's translation selects the strongest possible reading.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: responding to Crab's exact wording/date questions
text: A neutral translation would preserve the uncertainty.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: responding to Crab's exact wording/date questions
text: This annotation was added after the event, not during the ceremony.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: responding to Crab's exact wording/date questions
text: The correspondence after Mr. Swine left shows the claimant tightening its position.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: responding to Crab's exact wording/date questions
text: The document trail is doing what memory cannot do reliably.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Mr. Whimsy, language is not a fog machine, although in this case it has produced similar visibility.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: I would not call the clause a diplomatic eel in the pleading.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Your instinct is correct: ambiguity was used as pressure.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Your metaphor is memorable. The tribunal may prefer a sentence.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The phrase did not become binding because it wore ceremonial clothing.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Please do not describe the translation note as a sword. It is more like a measuring instrument.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: I appreciate the concern for fairness. I recommend fewer thunderclouds.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: A rights argument may help if tied to access, understanding, and fairness of process.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: If you say 'the pudding of obligation,' I will withdraw the note.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Precision can be dramatic enough.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Asia's index is excellent. It made the document set readable before anyone became poetic.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Please keep her version order. It is the most stable object in the case.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The tabs marked draft, signed version, invoice, clarification, and translation note should stay separate.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Good administration is often invisible until chaos arrives.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: If Asia had attended the ceremony, I suspect half this case would not exist.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The appointment log matches her chronology.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Please give her the clean copy. She will know where it belongs.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: I appreciate a file that does not require excavation.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Her notes distinguish language, authority, and quantum. That is exactly the structure needed.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Underpaid competence is the quiet infrastructure of justice.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: This is the Translation Note.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: It records the original phrase, a literal translation, and the range of reasonable legal meanings.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The claimant translates the phrase as a binding exclusive partnership. That is too strong without context.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: A more careful translation would say cooperative promotional arrangement or intention to explore cooperation.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The phrase may sound formal, but formality is not the same as final obligation.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The surrounding language refers to hospitality, promotion, and future coordination.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: It does not contain a clear price, duration, or enforceable scope.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The note should be used to show that the claimant's certainty is overstated.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: It also explains why Mr. Swine may have misunderstood the document, though he should still have read it.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: In short: ambiguity, not exoneration.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: I will provide the note on letterhead.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Please keep the explanatory paragraphs attached. A bare translation would be misleading.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The note is neutral. It assists the tribunal by preserving uncertainty.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Do not edit out the caveat that another reading is possible.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The caveat makes the note stronger, not weaker.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: A good expert note does not flatter the side that requested it.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The document should be disclosed with the original wording and prior draft.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: If the resort challenges it, ask them to provide their own contextual translation.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: I have included a paragraph on courtesy language and commercial commitment.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: There. Evidence, not rescue.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: follow-up comments after note is obtained
text: Do not make the note carry the authority argument. That is separate.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: follow-up comments after note is obtained
text: Do not make it carry the invoice argument. That is separate too.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: follow-up comments after note is obtained
text: Use it where the claimant says the language was obvious.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: follow-up comments after note is obtained
text: The note is strongest against overconfidence.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: follow-up comments after note is obtained
text: If paired with the Real Contract Draft, it becomes stronger.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: follow-up comments after note is obtained
text: If paired only with Mr. Swine's memory, it becomes weaker.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: follow-up comments after note is obtained
text: The note does not say he was careful. It says the phrase was not conclusive.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: follow-up comments after note is obtained
text: That distinction may save the argument from looking sentimental.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: follow-up comments after note is obtained
text: The tribunal will likely appreciate that the note does not exaggerate.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: follow-up comments after note is obtained
text: Neutral evidence often travels farther than convenient evidence.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The appointment log may assist the chronology.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Mr. Swine requested clarification before the full claim was formalised.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: That does not prove he was right. It proves he was uncertain before litigation strategy made uncertainty useful.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The timing matters because it shows concern before the invoice escalation.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: It is a small document, but small documents often fix large stories.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The log should be paired with his correspondence asking for clarification.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: It weakens any argument that his doubt was invented only after the claim.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: It also shows he did not simply ignore the issue.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Late concern is not ideal. Earlier than the claim is still relevant.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Use it carefully: it proves timing, not truth.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: I can provide a certified copy of the appointment entry.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The entry shows date, time, document reference, and the purpose: wording clarification.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: I have removed unrelated appointments for privacy.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The log is modest evidence, but clean.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Please do not make it into a dramatic revelation. It is a dated administrative record.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: It supports sequence.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Sequence matters here.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The appointment was not a legal consultation. It was a language clarification request.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: That distinction should remain clear.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Take the copy. Keep it with the chronology.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: This letter asks whether the language created binding commercial obligations.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: That question would be unnecessary if the meaning were as obvious as the claimant now says.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The reply avoided direct confirmation.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Avoidance can be important when one side later claims certainty.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The letter is polite, but the concern is visible.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Mr. Swine did not ask the ideal question at the ideal time. He did ask a relevant question.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The exchange shows ambiguity persisted after the event.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: It may support his credibility on misunderstanding.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: It may also show he recognised risk and moved too slowly.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Again: useful, not miraculous.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The earlier draft is important.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: It contains cooperation language but no exclusive payment obligation.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: It does not bind Pig & Swine to the service package now invoiced.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The later wording is materially firmer in some places.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: That change should be explained by the claimant.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: A party relying on an expanded obligation should show when and how that obligation was accepted.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The draft supports Mr. Swine's claim that he expected a softer document.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: It does not erase the signed text, but it changes how the signed text should be read.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The result is imperfect but survivable.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The tribunal accepted enough ambiguity to reduce the claim.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The evidence did not fully carry authority or quantum.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Mr. Swine's uncertainty damaged some of the stronger points.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Still, the full invoice did not stand.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: A weak victory is better than a broad obligation.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The remaining issues should be documented quickly.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Do not let relief erase the lessons.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Ambiguity helped, but carelessness cost something.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Next time, obtain translation before signing.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The argument became too broad.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Return to the distinctions: translation, authority, scope, quantum.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Do not claim the document means nothing.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Show that it does not clearly mean what the claimant says.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Use the earlier draft more carefully.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Use the appointment log to show doubt before the claim escalated.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Keep Mr. Swine's testimony narrow.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The tribunal needs disciplined uncertainty, not panic.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The Translation Note can still help if used precisely.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Try again with less rescue and more structure.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Please keep the full document set together.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Future disputes often begin when someone remembers only the useful half of an award.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Mr. Swine should not travel with signing authority until further notice.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Dr. A. Cula, you handled the ambiguity responsibly.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Mr. Murrow will likely convert this into office policy. Let him.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Asia should receive clean copies before anyone loses a ribbon.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Crab should keep the invoice analysis. It may be useful later.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Whimsy should not title the file The Pudding Accord.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The case is over only when the lesson has been filed.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: I hope our next meeting involves fewer desserts.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional Chapter 5 callback on multilingual/notice clarity
text: A notice that affects many people must still be understandable to each person.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional Chapter 5 callback on multilingual/notice clarity
text: Translation is not the only access issue. Plain language is also access.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional Chapter 5 callback on multilingual/notice clarity
text: If people need unofficial interpreters to know whether they are affected, the notice is weak.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional Chapter 5 callback on multilingual/notice clarity
text: Templates can help consistency. They can also spread the same defect everywhere.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional Chapter 5 callback on multilingual/notice clarity
text: The question is whether the recipient can understand consequence and remedy.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional Chapter 5 callback on multilingual/notice clarity
text: Ambiguity in a contract is costly. Ambiguity in a public notice can be structural.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional Chapter 5 callback on multilingual/notice clarity
text: If the process concerns legal-aid access, clarity becomes more important, not less.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional Chapter 5 callback on multilingual/notice clarity
text: A short deadline worsens unclear language.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional Chapter 5 callback on multilingual/notice clarity
text: People cannot exercise rights they cannot identify.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional Chapter 5 callback on multilingual/notice clarity
text: The court should ask whether the notice informed or merely announced.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional expert-style comment on access barriers
text: Language barriers do not only concern foreign languages.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional expert-style comment on access barriers
text: A public authority can write in the recipient's language and still fail to communicate.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional expert-style comment on access barriers
text: The notice uses formal terms without explaining practical consequences.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional expert-style comment on access barriers
text: For vulnerable recipients, unclear procedure can be as effective as denial.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional expert-style comment on access barriers
text: If the remedy route is difficult to understand, the remedy may not be real in practice.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional expert-style comment on access barriers
text: Mass communication should be tested against ordinary comprehension.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional expert-style comment on access barriers
text: The burden should not fall entirely on affected persons to decode risk.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional expert-style comment on access barriers
text: Where rights depend on timely objection, clarity is part of fairness.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional expert-style comment on access barriers
text: A process that assumes expert reading will exclude many people.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional expert-style comment on access barriers
text: Access to justice begins before court, when the person first understands what must be done.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional Supreme Court support on notice clarity
text: The notice should be judged by its practical intelligibility.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional Supreme Court support on notice clarity
text: A deadline is not meaningful if the obligation is unclear.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional Supreme Court support on notice clarity
text: The same unclear wording repeated widely creates systemic confusion.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional Supreme Court support on notice clarity
text: Consistency cannot cure a defect if the defect is consistent.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional Supreme Court support on notice clarity
text: The recipient needs to understand consequence, route, and evidence required.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional Supreme Court support on notice clarity
text: The formal availability of a remedy is not enough if the path is obscure.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional Supreme Court support on notice clarity
text: Plain language is not decoration. It is part of effective notice.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional Supreme Court support on notice clarity
text: The process should not require professional interpretation before rights can be exercised.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional Supreme Court support on notice clarity
text: Where many recipients are affected, testing clarity becomes more important.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
context: optional Supreme Court support on notice clarity
text: The court should distinguish administrative announcement from legally effective notice.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: I am glad Pig & Swine survived. Please do not make that sentence more dramatic than it is.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Mr. Swine has promised fewer ceremonial signatures. I have requested written confirmation.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Asia sent the final document bundle in perfect order. I was not surprised.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Whimsy's victory title was too long, but the legal point was sound.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Mr. Pig's maritime metaphor appears to have crossed into several jurisdictions.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Dr. A. Cula, you have developed a useful habit of asking what words actually do.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Bajtek's technical report was surprisingly clear. I suspect Asia edited the headings.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Attorney Grzyb's beige card was professionally moving, which is an unpleasant category.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: The final judgment may help more people than the parties expected.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: If anyone plans a celebration contract, send it to me first.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Ms. Tanaka continued producing translation notes that made overconfident contracts less dangerous.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Ms. Tanaka developed a short guide titled Politeness Is Not Always Authority.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Ms. Tanaka refused to let Mr. Swine sign anything after dessert without supervision.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Ms. Tanaka became Pig & Swine's preferred expert whenever words were doing too much work.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Ms. Tanaka quietly improved the Legal Quarter's multilingual notice templates.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Ms. Tanaka and Asia once reorganised a case file so efficiently that Mr. Murrow stood in respectful silence.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Ms. Tanaka still insists that ambiguity is not a miracle cure, only a fact to be handled honestly.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Ms. Tanaka remained professionally calm in the presence of pudding-related quantum disputes.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Ms. Tanaka's translation note became a training example for careful expert evidence.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.

speaker: Ms. Tanaka
text: Ms. Tanaka never allowed Whimsy to use the phrase diplomatic eel in a filed document.
notes: Voice-reference draft; not committed game text.
